title: The Fathers love for His Son. Part 4
scripture_text: ‘’Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John. But John tried to deter him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?” Jesus replied, “Let it be so now; it is proper for us to do this to fulfil all righteousness.” Then John consented. As soon as Jesus was baptized, he went up out of the water. At that moment heaven was opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting on him. And a voice from heaven said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.”
devotional:
3. The father also expressed his pleasure with his Son.

I find this amazing, God the Father did not think, ‘Jesus knows how I feel about him,’ He did not assume that his Son knew that he was loved. Authentic love and pleasure we find in others needs to be expressed. Expressing love is both a divine and human necessity. The word for pleased is eudokeo. In classic Greek it means to be well pleased or content, to be favoured.

It would be very easy to read this incident in the life of Jesus, and think, its a bit mushy or a bit sentimental. It does not sound very macho. But when we look a bit deeper and scratch beneath the surface we discover that when God the father spoke, he was alluding to three old testament passages.

In Psalm 2 7-9 we read ‘’7 I will proclaim the Lord’s decree: He said to me, “You are my son; today I have become your father. 8 Ask me, and I will make the nations your inheritance, the ends of the earth your possession. 9 You will break them with a rod of iron, you will dash them to pieces like pottery.”.

Psalm 2 is a messianic psalm which describes the privileges and responsibility of the Son.

The second passage that is alluded to his Genesis 22 look at what it says,

‘’Some time later God tested Abraham. He said to him, “Abraham!” “Here I am,” he replied. 2 Then God said, “Take your son, your only son, whom you love—Isaac—and go to the region of Moriah. Sacrifice him there as a burnt offering on a mountain I will show you.”

The third passage is Isaiah 42:1

“Here is my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen one in whom I delight.

Isaiah 42:1-4 is the first reference of four that refer to a suffering messiah in Isaiah 40-55. [see 46:1-6, 50:4-9, 52:13-53:12.].

As Jesus listened to his father speak, he not only heard affirming words of encouragement, but he also heard words of challenge. He knew that the affirming word that came from the old testament were also words that predicted his suffering and death.

The affirming love that God the father pours into our lives is the same love that produces courage and perseverance. The love through which God shows his pleasure is the same love that strengthens us for the suffering and sacrifices of the life. The life of Jesus teaches us that God the Father loves us and when we receive that love we are able to live the life that God has called us to.
prayer: Father I acknowledge I can’t do your will for my life without living the knowledge of your love.
html:
The Fathers love for His Son. Part 4

Study Text: Matthew 3 13-17
‘’Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John. But John tried to deter him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?” Jesus replied, “Let it be so now; it is proper for us to do this to fulfil all righteousness.” Then John consented. As soon as Jesus was baptized, he went up out of the water. At that moment heaven was opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting on him. And a voice from heaven said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.”

3. The father also expressed his pleasure with his Son.

I find this amazing, God the Father did not think, ‘Jesus knows how I feel about him,’ He did not assume that his Son knew that he was loved. Authentic love and pleasure we find in others needs to be expressed. Expressing love is both a divine and human necessity. The word for pleased is eudokeo. In classic Greek it means to be well pleased or content, to be favoured.

It would be very easy to read this incident in the life of Jesus, and think, its a bit mushy or a bit sentimental. It does not sound very macho. But when we look a bit deeper and scratch beneath the surface we discover that when God the father spoke, he was alluding to three old testament passages.

In Psalm 2 7-9 we read ‘’7 I will proclaim the Lord’s decree: He said to me, “You are my son; today I have become your father. 8 Ask me, and I will make the nations your inheritance, the ends of the earth your possession. 9 You will break them with a rod of iron, you will dash them to pieces like pottery.”.

Psalm 2 is a messianic psalm which describes the privileges and responsibility of the Son.

The second passage that is alluded to his Genesis 22 look at what it says,

‘’Some time later God tested Abraham. He said to him, “Abraham!” “Here I am,” he replied. 2 Then God said, “Take your son, your only son, whom you love—Isaac—and go to the region of Moriah. Sacrifice him there as a burnt offering on a mountain I will show you.”

The third passage is Isaiah 42:1

“Here is my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen one in whom I delight.

Isaiah 42:1-4 is the first reference of four that refer to a suffering messiah in Isaiah 40-55. [see 46:1-6, 50:4-9, 52:13-53:12.].

As Jesus listened to his father speak, he not only heard affirming words of encouragement, but he also heard words of challenge. He knew that the affirming word that came from the old testament were also words that predicted his suffering and death.

The affirming love that God the father pours into our lives is the same love that produces courage and perseverance. The love through which God shows his pleasure is the same love that strengthens us for the suffering and sacrifices of the life. The life of Jesus teaches us that God the Father loves us and when we receive that love we are able to live the life that God has called us to.

Prayer: Father I acknowledge I can’t do your will for my life without living the knowledge of your love.
Bible in a year reading plan: 2 Chronicles 19-21
